questions.text: What does 'terraform workspace new dev' do?

options.text: Creates a new variable file for dev environment
Creates a new workspace named 'dev' and switches to it
Creates a new directory for dev configuration
Initializes a new Terraform configuration for dev

questions.explanation: terraform workspace new <name> creates a new workspace with its own state and switches the current context to it. An empty state file is created. You can then run terraform plan/apply to create infrastructure in this workspace's state.

questions.text: What built-in variable contains the name of the current workspace?

options.text: var.workspace
local.workspace
terraform.workspace
env.workspace

questions.explanation: terraform.workspace is a built-in value that returns the name of the current workspace as a string. In the default workspace, it returns 'default'. Use it in expressions to create environment-specific configurations without separate configuration files.

questions.text: How does an S3 backend store state for multiple workspaces?

options.text: In separate S3 buckets per workspace
Under env:/<workspace>/<key> paths within the same bucket
As separate JSON objects in the same S3 file
In DynamoDB instead of S3

questions.explanation: With the S3 backend, workspace state is stored under env:/<workspace_name>/<configured_key>. The default workspace uses the configured key directly. This keeps all environment states in one bucket with clear naming: env:/staging/app/terraform.tfstate.

questions.text: When is using separate root configurations preferable to workspaces?

options.text: When you have more than 3 environments
When environments have fundamentally different architectures or are in different accounts
When using Terraform Cloud
Always — separate configs are always better than workspaces

questions.explanation: Workspaces work well when environments are structurally similar (same resources, just different sizes). Separate configurations are better when environments differ significantly — different AWS accounts, VPC layouts, compliance requirements, or when strict environment isolation is required.

questions.text: What does 'terraform workspace select production' do?

options.text: Creates the production workspace
Switches the active workspace to 'production'
Applies the production.tfvars file
Locks the production workspace

questions.explanation: terraform workspace select <name> switches the active workspace. Subsequent plan/apply operations will read from and write to that workspace's state. It does not create a workspace — use terraform workspace new for that.

questions.text: What is the name of the default workspace that exists in all new Terraform configurations?

options.text: main
default
root
base

questions.explanation: Every Terraform configuration starts with a single workspace named 'default'. You cannot delete the default workspace. When terraform.workspace returns 'default', you're in the initial workspace. This is the backward-compatible state location for configurations that predate workspaces.

questions.text: How can you prevent accidental destruction of production resources in a workspace?

options.text: Delete the production workspace when not in use
Use prevent_destroy lifecycle rule and workspace-conditional guard logic
Set workspace.readonly = true in backend config
Rename the workspace to 'protected-production'

questions.explanation: Use lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } on critical resources to block terraform destroy. You can also add conditional logic: if terraform.workspace == 'production' then instance_count = var.prod_count else instance_count = 1. Terraform Cloud/Enterprise also has policy-as-code (Sentinel) for workspace-level controls.

questions.text: Can you delete a workspace that has resources in its state?

options.text: Yes — it destroys the resources first
No — you must first destroy resources with terraform destroy
Yes — it archives the state for recovery
Yes — with terraform workspace delete -force

questions.explanation: terraform workspace delete <name> fails if the workspace has non-empty state. Use -force to override this safety check and delete the workspace with its state (leaving actual resources orphaned). Normally you should run terraform destroy first to clean up resources before deleting the workspace.

questions.text: What is a common pattern for workspace-specific variable values without -var-file?

options.text: terraform.auto.tfvars
A locals map keyed by terraform.workspace
Separate provider blocks per workspace
Using data sources to query workspace config

questions.explanation: A common pattern is to define a locals map keyed by workspace name: locals { config = { dev = {...}, staging = {...}, production = {...} }[terraform.workspace] }. This keeps all environment-specific values in one place and makes differences visible in code review.

options.text: All workspaces available in the Terraform Registry
All local workspaces with an asterisk (*) marking the current one
Only the current workspace
Available workspaces in Terraform Cloud

questions.explanation: terraform workspace list shows all workspaces for the current backend configuration. The currently active workspace is marked with an asterisk (*). For a new configuration, you'll see '* default' (default workspace, currently active).
